title: Sequential Measurements as a Resource for Quantum Metrology
authors: Koray Mentesoglu, Rahul Trivedi, Sara Mouradian
abstract: We present a protocol in which sequential weak measurements of a quantum harmonic oscillator enable simultaneous estimation of both quadratures of a displacement channel. Calculations of the quantum Fisher information show that the measurement backaction can increase the information gained for a range of measurement strengths. The protocol distributes information over a $N$-bit string after $N$ weak measurements. Thus we find that post-processing can be used to avoid information loss due to phase wrapping, increasing the effective dynamic range. Finally, the periodic information extraction makes the protocol robust to decoherence. Our results establish mid-sensing measurement as a resource for single- and multi-parameter quantum metrology.
summary: This paper proposes a protocol using sequential weak measurements to simultaneously estimate both quadratures of a quantum displacement. It demonstrates that this approach can achieve enhanced scaling of information, increase dynamic range, and remain robust against environmental decoherence.
main_problem: Developing a quantum metrology protocol for the simultaneous estimation of both quadratures of a displacement channel while overcoming limitations like decoherence and phase wrapping.
main_result: Sequential weak measurements act as a resource that can increase information gain via measurement backaction, achieve N^3 scaling of Quantum Fisher Information in certain regimes, and provide robustness to decoherence.
method: The authors use a sequential measurement protocol on an ancilla qubit coupled to a harmonic oscillator, employing a Dicke basis to reduce computational complexity and analyzing information via Quantum and Classical Fisher Information.
model_or_system: A Quantum Harmonic Oscillator (QHO) coupled to an ancilla qubit, subject to a displacement channel and subject to environmental decoherence (loss and dephasing) modeled by a Lindblad master equation.
key_observables: Measurement outcomes of the ancilla qubit (N-bit string), Hamming weights, and Quantum/Classical Fisher Information.
important_parameters: Displacement parameters (beta_1, beta_2), measurement strength (alpha), number of measurement rounds (N), and effective decay rate (Gamma).
main_assumptions: The thermal occupancy of the oscillator is assumed to be negligible (n_th approx 0), and the protocol assumes the QHO cannot be measured directly.
figures_summary: Fig 1 illustrates the single vs. sequential measurement protocols; Fig 2 shows QFI superiority and scaling exponents; Fig 3 compares single vs. two-parameter performance; Fig 4 shows CFI dips; Fig 7 shows the monotonic decrease of CRB in the presence of decoherence; Fig 8 compares joint-system QFI.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the sequential protocol, establishes the mathematical framework using the Dicke basis, compares scaling regimes (weak vs. strong measurement), extends the protocol to two-parameter estimation, analyzes robustness against decoherence, and discusses connections to grid states.
why_interesting: It provides a novel way to use measurement backaction as a resource and demonstrates how sequentiality can fundamentally change the scaling of precision and robustness to noise in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a A QHO feels a force f(t), which manifests as a displacement in phase space. A single interrogation time consists of N signal interactions. b Single Measurement Protocol: Phase is accumulated in the QHO to gain a factor of N advantage in precision. The signal displacement ˆD(β) is followed by an ancilla-assisted measurement (orange box) which includes a state-dependent coupling displacement and projective measurement on the qubit. c Sequential Measurement Protocol: The sequential-measurement protocol repeats the coupling and measurement operations at every round. The qubit is reset into the ground state, |↓⟩, before the next round, but QHO evolution continues.

FIG. 3. QFI comparison at N = 100 and |α| = 0.2. In light blue, single-parameter estimation using N measurements. In blue, single-parameter estimation using 2N measurements. In dashed dark blue, two-parameter estimation using 2N mea- surements plotting only FQ 11. The single-parameter curves share a similar shape, while the two-parameter curve in- cludes an increased range of optimal |α|. Inset: Though 2N measurements for both protocols yield comparable informa- tion gain for FQ 11, the plotted two-parameter protocol also has information about the other parameter FQ 22, whereas the single-parameter protocol only gives information about a sin- gle quadrature.

FIG. 2. a QFI vs. |α| for N ∈{410, 450, 500} for the single- (orange) and sequential- (blue) measurement protocols. The sequential-measurement protocol exhibits larger information gain and a wider range of optimal |α|. Increasing N will in- crease the overall FQ gain. b Heat map of scaling exponent, p = d log FQ/d log N, for different measurement strengths and N. An example contour, p = 2.25, is plotted. c Scal- ing exponent p plotted as a function of N|α|2 for increasing N. The weak and strong measurement regimes are defined by N|α|2 ≪1 and N|α|2 ≫1.

FIG. 4. Using |α| = 0.2 a FC/FQ is plotted as a function of signal interactions, N, for performing single-parameter es- timation of two different signals: β1 = 0.05 and β1 = 0.1. The CFI saturates the QFI. Larger β1 exhibits dips in infor- mation gain. b Average excitation number ⟨k⟩= P

FIG. 5. Using |α| = 0.2 a The CFI for β1 = 0.1 for the sequential- (blue) and single- (orange) measurement proto- cols. The sequential measurements present a fundamentally different method for information gain, allowing signal infor- mation to be recovered, while avoiding interference dips (dot- ted line). b Sequential- and c single-measurement protocol outcome probability distributions for N = 40 at two signal strengths. At β1 = 0.05 (top row), both protocols produce distributions skewed towards the ground states. At β1 = 0.1 (bottom row), the distributions become symmetric, corre- sponding to the maximum-slope point in Fig.4(b), where sen- sitivity to β1 is greatest.
